blocks.content:
System Design Interview
Common Patterns — How to Build Core Functionalities
These are the reusable building blocks that appear across dozens of system design problems. Master these patterns
and you can compose any system.
Pattern 1: File Upload to S3 (Multipart)
Used in: YouTube, Dropbox, Instagram, WhatsApp, Yelp, Netflix, Strava
The standard pattern for uploading any file larger than a few MB. Handles failures, resumes, and scales to any file
size.
Core Flow
1
Client requests upload → Upload Service creates S3 multipart upload → gets uploadId + presigned URLs per
chunk
2
Client uploads chunks in parallel to presigned URLs; S3 returns ETag per chunk
3
Connection drops? Call S3 ListParts(uploadId) → find missing chunks → re-upload only those
4
All chunks uploaded → client calls CompleteMultipartUpload → S3 assembles single object
5
S3 fires s3:ObjectCreated event → Upload Service receives notification → saves final URL to DB
Post-Upload Processing (Async Workers)
 Quarantine bucket: all uploads land here first; virus scan + content validation before moving to public bucket
 Rechunker worker: re-chunks file per system needs (video frames, audio segments)
 Transcoder worker: produces multiple quality variants (240p/480p/720p/1080p/4K)
 Manifest file: references all chunks + quality variants; uploaded to CDN for popular content
 Thumbnail generator: Lambda triggered on upload; resizes to multiple dimensions; stores back in S3
Key Rules
 Always include file size limits in presigned URL conditions — prevents storage bombs
 Store uploadId in localStorage so uploads survive app restarts
 Set S3 lifecycle policy to auto-delete incomplete uploads after 1–2 days
 Use range requests (HTTP Range header) for resumable downloads
Pattern: Client → Upload Service (get presigned URLs) → S3 (direct upload) → S3 event → Worker → DB update
Pattern 2: Media Serving (CDN + Adaptive Bitrate)
Used in: YouTube, Netflix, Instagram, Dropbox, Yelp, WhatsApp
Once files are in S3, serve them efficiently at global scale.

Core Flow
1
Client requests media → CDN edge node checks cache
2
Cache hit: serve from edge (<5ms latency)
3
Cache miss: CDN fetches from S3 origin → caches at edge → serves to client
4
For video: client fetches HLS/DASH manifest → selects quality based on network → fetches chunks
5
Client adapts quality in real-time (adaptive bitrate) — switches to lower quality on slow connection
Serving Strategies
 Static assets (images, documents): S3 + CloudFront CDN; set long cache TTL (1 year) with content-hash in
URL
 Video streaming: HLS (.m3u8 manifest) or DASH (.mpd manifest); 2–10 second segments; CDN caches
segments
 Adaptive bitrate: manifest lists all quality variants; client player selects based on bandwidth estimate
 Presigned URLs: time-limited signed URLs for private content; CDN can also use signed cookies
 Range requests: HTTP Range header enables resumable downloads and seeking in video
Rule: Serve everything through CDN. Ensure range requests work for large files. Let CDN + browser handle
optimization.
Pattern 3: Real-Time Updates (WebSocket / SSE)
Used in: WhatsApp, FB Live Comments, Lyft tracking, LeetCode results, Ticketmaster, Strava, Robinhood
Push data from server to client without polling.
Protocol Selection
 SSE (Server-Sent Events): one-way server→client over HTTP; ideal for feeds, scores, notifications, AI
streaming
 WebSocket: bidirectional; use for chat, collaborative editing, live tracking, multiplayer
 Long Polling: client waits for response; simple but inefficient; use only for low-frequency updates
 WebRTC: peer-to-peer; use for video/voice calls and large file transfers
Scaling WebSocket/SSE Servers
1
Client connects to any server instance
2
Instance queries Zookeeper/Redis for correct instance mapping
3
If wrong instance: redirect client to correct one
4
Instance subscribes to Kafka topic (e.g., by videoId or userId) for relevant events
5
Events arrive via Kafka → instance pushes to connected clients via WebSocket/SSE

6
Instance failure: Zookeeper updates mapping; clients reconnect to new instance
Offline Message Delivery
 Store messages in per-user Redis queue (list) with TTL = 30 days
 On reconnect: flush all queued messages in order
 For mobile offline: APNs (iOS) / FCM (Android) push notifications
Pattern: Kafka (durable event log) → WebSocket/SSE servers (fan-out to connected clients) → Redis queue (offline
buffer)
Pattern 4: Geospatial Queries (Location Services)
Used in: Uber, Lyft (all 4 services), Yelp, Delivery System, Tinder, Strava
Find nearby entities (drivers, businesses, users) efficiently at scale.
Storage & Query Options
 Redis GEOADD/GEORADIUS: real-time positions; <5ms proximity queries; shard by geohash prefix
 PostGIS ST_DWithin: accurate radius queries; source of truth; combine with text/rating filters
 Elasticsearch geo_point: combine geo + full-text + rating in one query; sync from PostgreSQL via Kafka
 Geohash cells: partition key for sharding; cache key for ETAs; surge zone boundaries
Real-Time Driver Tracking Flow
1
Driver sends GPS every 4s → Location API validates → Redis GEOADD (sharded by geohash prefix)
2
Location API publishes to Kafka → Cassandra (24h history) + Rider Tracking Service
3
Rider requests nearby drivers → Proximity Service → Redis GEORADIUS on target cell + 8 neighbors
4
Active ride: Tracking Service subscribes to Kafka by driver_id → pushes to rider via WebSocket
5
Driver offline (30s no heartbeat) → background job removes from Redis geospatial index
Geohash Sharding
 Shard Redis by geohash prefix (4 chars ≈ 40km cells)
 Proximity query fans out to target cell + 8 neighbors (handles boundary cases)
 Fan-out is parallelized → completes in <10ms
 Apply Kalman filter to smooth GPS noise (±10m) before storing
Pattern: Redis Geo (real-time index, sharded by geohash) + Cassandra (history) + Kafka (streaming to riders)
Pattern 5: Search (Full-Text + Geo + Semantic)
Used in: Yelp, YouTube, FB Post Search, News Aggregator, Web Crawler, Ticketmaster
Multi-dimensional search combining text relevance, location, and ranking signals.

Search Stack
1
PostgreSQL: source of truth for all business/content data (ACID, joins)
2
Kafka CDC (Debezium): streams every DB change to Kafka in real-time
3
Elasticsearch consumer: updates search index from Kafka; lag <1 second
4
Search query: geo_distance filter + BM25 text relevance + function score (rating, recency)
5
For semantic search: pre-compute embeddings → store in Faiss vector DB → merge with BM25 results
6
Re-rank merged results using learned ranking model (CTR, watch time, freshness)
Ranking Signals (Yelp/YouTube)
 Relevance: Elasticsearch BM25 score for text match
 Distance: closer businesses/content ranked higher
 Rating: higher-rated entities ranked higher
 Review/view count: more engagement = more trustworthy
 Recency: recently updated content ranked higher
 Promoted: Elasticsearch function_score multiplies relevance for paid listings
Autocomplete
 Elasticsearch prefix queries on analyzed fields
 Or: Redis sorted set with ZADD score=0 + lexicographic ordering (ZRANGEBYLEX)
 Cache top-N suggestions per prefix in Redis
Pattern: PostgreSQL (truth) → Kafka CDC → Elasticsearch (search) + Faiss (semantic) → Redis (cache results)
Pattern 6: Feed Generation (Fan-Out)
Used in: FB News Feed, Instagram, Twitter/X, Google News, News Aggregator
Generate personalized feeds for millions of users with low read latency.
Fan-Out on Write (Push) — Recommended for most users
1
User creates post → Post Service writes to DB → publishes to Kafka
2
Fan-out workers consume Kafka → for each follower, prepend post_id to their Redis feed list
3
Read: GET user:{id}:feed → O(1) Redis list read → fetch post details in batch
4
Feed is pre-built; read latency is sub-millisecond
Fan-Out on Read (Pull) — For celebrities
 At read time: fetch posts from all followed accounts and merge
 Use for accounts with >1M followers to avoid fan-out storm

 Merge celebrity posts into feed at read time (hybrid approach)
Hybrid Pattern (Recommended)
 Push to followers with <1000 followers (regular users)
 Pull from celebrities (>1M followers) at read time and merge
 Use Bloom filters to skip already-seen content
 Batch fan-out workers: process followers in groups of 100 per Kafka message
Pattern: Kafka (post events) → Fan-out workers → Redis feed lists (pre-computed) + pull for celebrities at read time
Pattern 7: Counting at Scale (Views, Likes, Clicks)
Used in: YouTube, Ad Click Aggregator, Instagram, FB News Feed, Robinhood, Strava
Count events at massive scale without overwhelming the database.
Counter Architecture
1
Event arrives (click, view, like) → API validates → publishes to Kafka
2
Stream processor (Flink/Spark) aggregates events in memory (1-minute windows)
3
Flink writes aggregated summaries to DB (not raw events) — 1000x write reduction
4
For real-time display: Redis INCR per entity; flush to DB every 60 seconds
5
For viral content: shard counter across N Redis keys; sum at read time
Deduplication
 Client sends UUID per event (click, view)
 Server caches UUID in Redis for 5 seconds; discard duplicates
 For stream processing: Flink watermarking + unique event IDs
Approximate vs. Exact Counts
 Approximate (HyperLogLog): count unique users with <1% error using 12KB memory
 Exact: store all event IDs; expensive at scale
 YouTube approach: display rounded counts; delay by a few hours to prevent gaming
Pattern: Kafka (ingest) → Flink (aggregate in-memory) → DB (summaries) + Redis (real-time counters)
Pattern 8: Distributed Locking & Contention
Used in: Uber, Lyft Matching, Online Auction, Ticketmaster, Tinder, Payment System
Prevent race conditions when multiple processes compete for the same resource.
Redis Distributed Lock (SETNX)

1
Process tries to acquire lock: SET lock:{resource_id} {owner_id} NX EX {ttl_seconds}
2
If SET returns OK: lock acquired; proceed with critical section
3
If SET returns nil: lock held by another process; retry with backoff or move to next candidate
4
After critical section: DEL lock:{resource_id} (only if owner_id matches — use Lua script)
5
TTL ensures lock is released even if process crashes
Two-Phase Matching Pattern (Uber/Lyft)
 Phase 1: Redis GEORADIUS → find top 5 candidate drivers (<5ms)
 Phase 2: Score candidates (proximity + rating + ETA); try to acquire Redis lock on driver_id
 Lock acquired → write match to DB → notify driver + rider
 Lock failed (driver already assigned) → move to next candidate
 No driver found in 30s → expand radius by 50% and retry
Reservation Pattern (Ticketmaster)
 User selects seat → soft lock (TTL=10min) placed on seat in Redis
 Seat appears 'held' to other users during TTL window
 User completes checkout → hard lock (DB transaction) converts soft lock to booking
 TTL expires without checkout → seat released back to available pool
Rule: Use Redis SETNX for distributed locks. Always set TTL. Use Lua script for atomic check-and-release.
Pattern 9: Async Processing Pipeline
Used in: LeetCode, Ad Click Aggregator, Job Scheduler, YouTube transcoding, WhatsApp media
Decouple request acceptance from heavy processing. Never make the user wait for a DB write.
Core Flow
1
API receives request → validates → drops into Kafka/SQS → returns 202 Accepted immediately
2
Worker pool consumes from queue → processes job (transcode, execute code, send email)
3
Worker publishes result to result queue → Result Service pushes to client via WebSocket/SSE
4
On failure: retry up to N times → move to Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) for investigation
5
Autoscale workers based on queue depth (not CPU) — CloudWatch alarm → Auto Scaling group
Reliability Patterns
 Idempotency keys: deduplicate jobs; safe to retry
 Heartbeat: worker pings queue every N seconds; missed heartbeat → re-queue job
 DLQ: after 3–5 failures, isolate bad jobs; healthy work continues

 Backpressure: return 503 when queue depth exceeds threshold
 Pre-warmed pools: keep N execution environments ready (LeetCode: 10 microVMs per language per node)
Pattern: API (accept + enqueue) → Kafka → Workers (process) → Result queue → WebSocket push to client
Pattern 10: File Sync Across Devices (Dropbox Pattern)
Used in: Dropbox, Google Docs
Detect local changes and propagate to remote; detect remote changes and apply locally.
Local → Remote Sync
1
OS file watcher (FSEvents on macOS, FileSystemWatcher on Windows) detects file change
2
Sync agent queues modified file for upload
3
Sync agent calls Sync API with file chunks + updated metadata
4
Sync Service uploads file to S3 (multipart) + updates metadata DB
5
Sync Service publishes change event to Kafka → notifies other online clients via SSE
Remote → Local Sync
1
Client B is online: Sync Service sends SSE notification about change
2
Client B calls Sync API to get all changes since last sync timestamp
3
Client B compares remote metadata with local file system
4
Client B downloads only changed files from S3 (using presigned URLs)
5
Client B is offline: on reconnect, queries Sync Service for all changes since last_sync_at
Conflict Resolution
 Last write wins: most recent edit (by server timestamp) is saved
 Vector clocks: track causality; detect concurrent edits; present conflict to user
 Operational Transform (OT): Google Docs approach; merge concurrent edits mathematically
Pattern: OS watcher → Sync Agent → S3 (storage) + Sync Service (metadata) → Kafka → SSE to other clients
Pattern 11: Rate Limiting
Used in: Rate Limiter, Ticketmaster, API Gateway, Ad Click Aggregator
Protect services from abuse and overload.
Token Bucket (Recommended)
1
Each user has a bucket with capacity N tokens; bucket refills at rate R tokens/second

2
Each request consumes 1 token; if bucket empty → reject with 429 Too Many Requests
3
Store bucket state in Redis: key = rate_limit:{user_id}, value = {tokens, last_refill_time}
4
Use Lua script for atomic check-and-decrement (prevents race conditions)
5
Return Retry-After header with seconds until next token available
Distributed Rate Limiting
 Use consistent hashing to route user_id to same Redis node
 For multi-region: use Redis cluster with cross-region replication (slight inconsistency acceptable)
 Sliding window counter: blend current + previous window counts for smooth limiting
 Different limits per tier: free (100 req/min), pro (1000 req/min), enterprise (unlimited)
Pattern: API Gateway → Redis (token bucket, Lua atomic) → 429 if exceeded → Retry-After header
Pattern 12: Payment Processing
Used in: Payment System, Uber, Online Auction, Ticketmaster, Robinhood
Handle money movement with exactly-once semantics and full auditability.
Core Flow
1
Client submits payment with idempotency key (UUID) in header
2
Payment Service checks idempotency store (Redis/DB) — if key exists, return cached result
3
Call payment processor (Stripe/Braintree) with idempotency key forwarded
4
On success: write to ledger DB (append-only) + publish PaymentCompleted event to Kafka
5
On failure: Saga compensating transaction — refund if downstream service failed
Key Principles
 Idempotency keys: prevent double-charge on retry; forward to payment processor
 Append-only ledger: never update payment records; only append new entries
 Outbox pattern: write payment record + event in same DB transaction
 Saga pattern: if order creation fails after payment, trigger refund compensating transaction
 Exactly-once: use idempotency keys + deduplication store; not guaranteed by queue alone
Pattern: Idempotency key → Payment processor → Append-only ledger → Kafka event → Saga for rollback
Pattern 13: Collaborative Real-Time Editing (Google Docs)
Used in: Google Docs, Strava (activity sharing)
Multiple users editing the same document simultaneously without conflicts.

Operational Transform (OT)
 Each edit is an operation (insert char at position X, delete char at position Y)
 When two concurrent operations conflict, transform one against the other before applying
 Server is the authority: all operations pass through server; server applies OT and broadcasts
 Client applies operations optimistically; server may send corrected version
CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type)
 Alternative to OT; operations are designed to be commutative and idempotent
 No central server needed for conflict resolution; works offline
 More complex to implement but better for offline-first applications
Architecture
1
User types → client generates operation → sends via WebSocket to Document Service
2
Document Service applies OT → updates document state → broadcasts to all connected clients
3
Document state persisted to DB every N seconds (not on every keystroke)
4
New user joins → receives current document snapshot + operation log since snapshot
5
Cursor positions broadcast via WebSocket for presence awareness
Pattern: WebSocket (bidirectional) → OT server (conflict resolution) → broadcast to all clients → periodic DB
snapshot
Pattern 14: Notification System
Used in: WhatsApp, FB News Feed, Ticketmaster, Price Tracker, Strava, Instagram
Deliver notifications reliably across multiple channels.
Multi-Channel Delivery Flow
1
Event occurs (new message, price drop, match) → published to Kafka
2
Notification Service consumes Kafka → looks up user preferences + device tokens
3
Route to appropriate channel: WebSocket (online), APNs/FCM (mobile offline), Email, SMS
4
Track delivery status per channel; retry failed deliveries with exponential backoff
5
User reads notification → mark as read; update unread count in Redis
Channel Selection Logic
 User is online (WebSocket connected): push via WebSocket immediately
 User is offline (mobile): APNs (iOS) or FCM (Android) push notification
 User is offline (email): batch email digest (hourly/daily) to reduce noise

 Critical alerts (price drop, security): all channels simultaneously
 User preferences: respect do-not-disturb settings and channel opt-outs
Pattern: Kafka event → Notification Service → channel router → WebSocket / APNs / FCM / Email
Pattern 15: Metrics & Monitoring Pipeline
Used in: Metrics Monitoring, Ad Click Aggregator, Lyft ETA accuracy, YouTube view counts
Collect, aggregate, and alert on system metrics at scale.
Metrics Pipeline
1
Agents on each host collect metrics (CPU, memory, latency, error rate, queue depth)
2
Agents push metrics to Kafka (high-throughput ingest)
3
Stream processor (Flink) aggregates: 1-minute averages, P99 latency, error rates
4
Aggregated metrics written to time-series DB (Prometheus, TimescaleDB, InfluxDB)
5
Alerting rules evaluate metrics; trigger PagerDuty/OpsGenie on threshold breach
6
Dashboards (Grafana) query time-series DB for visualization
Key Metrics to Track
 Queue depth: primary autoscaling signal (not CPU)
 P99 latency: tail latency matters more than average
 Cache hit rate: low hit rate signals caching strategy problem
 Replication lag: for read replicas and CDC pipelines
 Error rate: 5xx rate; distinguish client errors (4xx) from server errors (5xx)
 ETA accuracy: predicted vs. actual for routing services
Pattern: Agents → Kafka → Flink (aggregate) → Time-series DB → Grafana dashboards + PagerDuty alerts
